column2: Vintage Bronze Sculpture Ombra della Sera, Italy 1950-70s
column3:
This captivating vintage metal sculpture with bronze verdigris patina pays homage to the legendary Etruscan votive statuette Ombra della Sera (“Shadow of the Evening”), an iconic 3rd-century BCE bronze from Volterra's Museo Etrusco Guarnacci. Depicting a nude male youth with dramatically elongated, filiform limbs and torso—yet a realistically proportioned head—the piece evokes the original's enigmatic silhouette, said to resemble a human shadow stretched by sunset light.

This mid-20th-century reproduction (Italy, circa 1950s–1970s) bridges ancient mystery and modernist abstraction—often linked to Alberto Giacometti's influence from the same Etruscan source.

The piece is in very good vintage condition. The sculpture partly has the original's label.

Depth: 10 cm / 3.9 inches

column2: Large Bronze Wall Candle Sconce, France 1950s
column3:
Beautiful French wall sconce/candleholder made of gilded bronze, made in the form of tied ribbons supporting two candleholders ending with tassels wrapped around buttons decorated with floral rosettes.
These neoclassical elements, typical of the Louis XVI style, refer to the Greek and Roman cultures.
The bronze casting is made of very high quality, with clear details and a pleasant, natural golden patina formed from time to time. Very often, such sconces are designed for use with electric lighting — the same one has not been changed and is still intended for use as a candleholder. Classic and elegant in form, it will bring an element of romantic antiquity to any interior.

The wall candle sconce is in good vintage condition.

column2: Mid-century Sputnik chandelier by Fontana Arte, Italy 1970s
column3:
Vintage art glass chandelier Sputnik by Fontana Arte made in Italy in the 1970s.
Fontana Arte was founded in 1932 by Luigi Fontana and Gio Ponti. Luigi Fontana came from a family with a long history in the glass industry, and Gio Ponti was an architect and designer. Together, they aimed to blend traditional Italian craftsmanship with modern design principles. Fontana Arte is known for its innovative and forward-thinking design approach. Their creations often feature clean lines, geometric shapes, and a focus on functionality, making them emblematic of mid-century modern design.
Crafted using the unique "serrated" (or "scapellato" in Italian) glass-cutting technique, this chandelier features an array of meticulously hand-cut and hand-chipped glass rhombuses, artfully arranged in a spherical formation.

column2: Italian Glass Smalti Mosaic – Christ Pantocrator Icon, Mid-20th Century
column3:
Italian Glass Smalti Mosaic – Christ Pantocrator Icon, Mid-20th Century

Decorative mosaic panel depicting Christ Pantocrator, made with hand-cut glass smalti tesserae set into a solid plaster base and framed in wood. The surface is composed of irregular mosaic pieces carefully placed to follow the lines of the face and drapery, creating depth and subtle movement typical of traditional mosaic work.

The halo and garments are built from richly colored glass pieces that catch light from different angles, giving the panel a vibrant texture. The technique reflects the long Italian mosaic tradition inspired by Byzantine church mosaics.

Made in Italy around the 1960s–1970s as a devotional or decorative wall piece. Heavy construction due to the plaster backing and glass tesserae.
A striking vintage religious artwork with strong texture and presence, suitable for both traditional and eclectic interiors.

column2: Vintage chandelier by Pietro Chiesa for Fontana Arte, Italy 1930s
column3:
Vintage disk chandelier by Pietro Chiesa for Fontana Arte made in Italy in the 1930s.
Pietro Chiesa, a prominent figure in Italian Modernist design, left an indelible mark on the world of artistic glass furnishings. Born in Milan in 1892 and passing away in Paris in 1948, Chiesa's journey began with formal training at the Accademia di Belle Arti di Brera, followed by an apprenticeship under the esteemed furniture designer Giovanni Battista Gianotti. Establishing his own studio, the Bottega di Pietro Chiesa, in Milan in 1921, Chiesa swiftly gained recognition, showcasing his work at the influential 1925 Paris Exposition. A pivotal moment came in 1932 when he was invited by Gio Ponti to join forces with Luigi Fontana SA, a leading manufacturer of artistic stained-glass products in Italy. Together with Ponti and Luigi Fontana, Chiesa assumed the role of artistic director, merging his studio with Fontana SA to form Fontana Arte in 1933. Under Chiesa's creative leadership, Fontana Arte flourished, producing a plethora of innovative designs that epitomized the pinnacle of glass craftsmanship and modern material techniques. Among his iconic creations are the groundbreaking Fontana table, crafted from a single band of bent clear glass, the striking Cartoccio vase, and the timeless lacquered brass Luminator floor lamp. While Chiesa's designs exude a distinct modernity, they also reflect his reverence for classical forms, demonstrating a seamless fusion of tradition and innovation. Additionally, Chiesa's masterful use of color, notably deep blue and green, became synonymous with Fontana Arte's aesthetic during his tenure. column2: Murano Glass Pendant Lamp: Mid-Century Modern Italian Lighting by Leucos, 1970s
column3:
Discover the timeless beauty of the Samanta Pendant Lamp, a masterpiece of Italian design by Roberto Pamio, brought to life by Leucos in Salzano, Italy in 1971.

This iconic lamp, a true symbol of 20th-century design, continues to captivate with its blend of sophistication and functionality.

Roberto Pamio, a distinguished architect and designer, envisioned the Samanta Pendant Lamp. Inspired by his studies under renowned mentors such as Carlo Scarpa and Bruno Zevi, Pamio's design showcases a harmonious fusion of aesthetics and architectural precision. This pendant lamp boasts a unique trumpet-shaped lampshade, meticulously crafted from two parts of Murano glass. The upper section features opal hand-blown glass, providing soft, diffused illumination. Below, a clear glass lampshade with a striking red rim adds a touch of artistry. Crafted in the heart of Italy, this lamp exemplifies Italian craftsmanship at its finest. It reflects a legacy of artistry and attention to detail, making it a cherished addition to any interior. The Samanta Pendant Lamp's design transcends time and trends. Its enduring elegance and versatility allow it to seamlessly complement various interior styles, from mid-century modern to contemporary aesthetics.

Equipped with a standard E27 bulb socket, this pendant lamp is easy to install, bringing a touch of Italian design excellence to your space.

 Illuminate your home or workspace with the Samanta Pendant Lamp and experience the enduring allure of Italian design. Elevate your interior with this iconic piece that transcends generations and adds a touch of architectural brilliance to your surroundings.

Chandelier height with cable 125 cm. The chandelier weight 5.5 kg.

column2: Vintage Chrome Floor Lamp by Adalberto Dal Lago for Esperia, 1960s Italian Lighting
column3:
The mid-century modern floor lamp by Adalberto Dal Lago for Esperia was made in Italy in the 1960s. This attractive item is crafted from chromed metal and perspex. The lampshade and base have metal rods around, which gives it a unique look, especially when turned on. It will be a great addition to any modern interior.

Excellent vintage condition. EU plug, requires 3 standard bulbs, Edison E14. 

  • Width: 45 cm
  • Height: 113 cm

Born in Milan in 1937, Adalberto Dal Lago holds a degree in architecture from the Politecnico di Milano, where he began his academic career first as an assistant professor of decoration and then as an assistant professor and lecturer in the elements of composition. He published many works on architecture, design and decoration, and also edited the magazine "Rassegna-Modi di abitare oggi" and the "Technology/Design" section of the magazine "L'architettura-Cronache e Storia" published by Bruno Zevi. He has been a member of the jury of Italian and international competitions and has presented papers at many congresses and conferences in Europe; he also curated many architecture and design exhibitions. As an architect, he has designed the exteriors and interiors of buildings of all kinds: private houses, residential and public complexes, industrial buildings, research centers, public and private offices, hotels, restaurants and exhibition halls. As an industrial designer, Adalberto Dal Lago is a longtime consultant to some of the world's leading home furnishing, textile, vinyl and ceramics companies and is also responsible for color trends in a wide range of automotive design and styling sectors and motorcycles. His graphic design work ranges from single product projects such as logos, packaging and catalogs to coordinating corporate image, advertising and publishing projects for architecture, furniture and design firms. (Literature: Marazzigruppe).

column2: Vintage Fontana Arte Disk Chandelier by Pietro Chiesa, 1940s Italian Lighting
column3:
Vintage disk chandelier by Pietro Chiesa for Fontana Arte made in Italy in the 1940s.

 Pietro Chiesa, a prominent figure in Italian Modernist design, left an indelible mark on the world of artistic glass furnishings. Born in Milan in 1892 and passing away in Paris in 1948, Chiesa's journey began with formal training at the Accademia di Belle Arti di Brera, followed by an apprenticeship under the esteemed furniture designer Giovanni Battista Gianotti. Establishing his own studio, the Bottega di Pietro Chiesa, in Milan in 1921, Chiesa swiftly gained recognition, showcasing his work at the influential 1925 Paris Exposition. A pivotal moment came in 1932 when he was invited by Gio Ponti to join forces with Luigi Fontana SA, a leading manufacturer of artistic stained-glass products in Italy. Together with Ponti and Luigi Fontana, Chiesa assumed the role of artistic director, merging his studio with Fontana SA to form Fontana Arte in 1933. Under Chiesa's creative leadership, Fontana Arte flourished, producing a plethora of innovative designs that epitomized the pinnacle of glass craftsmanship and modern material techniques. Among his iconic creations are the groundbreaking Fontana table, crafted from a single band of bent clear glass, the striking Cartoccio vase, and the timeless lacquered brass Luminator floor lamp. While Chiesa's designs exude a distinct modernity, they also reflect his reverence for classical forms, demonstrating a seamless fusion of tradition and innovation. Additionally, Chiesa's masterful use of color, notably deep blue and green, became synonymous with Fontana Arte's aesthetic during his tenure. Through his visionary approach and dedication to excellence, Pietro Chiesa cemented his legacy as a trailblazer in the realm of Italian design, leaving behind a rich tapestry of timeless creations that continue to inspire and captivate admirers worldwide.

Three exquisitely sculpted and sandblasted glass discs interact with light in contrasting manners, offering a captivating display of luminosity and shadow. Crafted from flawless glass. Polished brass components exhibit pristine clarity.

Electrical cables have been renewed to professional electrician.

column3:
The rare large hand-blown Murano glass pendant lamp, designed by Ettore Fantasia and Gino Poli for Sotis Murano, Italy, 60s. The "pulegoso" glass is made using a method that was often used in the 1920s, with the addition of gasoline to thereby cause the glass to boil in a melt. Countless tiny air bubbles of irregular shape inside the glass mass deliberately limit its transparency. "Lattimo" is a special glass processing. This is a white milky-like glass in which the opacity is provided by the presence of microcrystals. The crystals change the index of refraction, thus giving the glass mass a milky appearance.

column2: Antique silver plated tray by Queen Victoria Sheffield, England, 1900s
column3:
The antique tray marked "Queen Victoria Sheffield" belongs to the English tradition of producing tableware and serving items in the style of the Victorian era. Such items were produced in Sheffield, the famous center of silver production. Judging by the stamp with the inscription "Queen Victoria" and the image of the coat of arms, this product is from the so—called "Sheffield Plate" collection - a technique that included coating copper or brass objects with silver. The brand on the teapot, such as the image of a crown, a coat of arms with a lion and other symbols, are typical for British silverware manufacturers.

The antique tray has the original manufacturer's label.

column2: [Reserved, included shipping] Murano pendant lamp Egg by Leucos, Italy 1960s Mid-century italian modern lighting
column3:
Introduce a touch of mid-century Italian modern elegance into your space with the Murano pendant lamp "Egg" by Leucos, originating from Italy in the 1960s. This striking lamp features a distinctive egg-shaped design, transitioning seamlessly from white glass to opalescent glass, creating a mesmerizing visual effect.

 Adorned with its original label, this pendant lamp exudes authenticity and heritage. While in excellent vintage condition overall, it's worth noting that there are a couple of minor cracks on the ceiling plastic canopy. Illuminate your interior with the timeless allure of mid-century Italian design, as this Egg pendant lamp casts a warm and inviting glow, adding both style and sophistication to any room.

Leucos is an Italian lighting company with a rich history dating back to the 1960s. Founded in 1962 in Scorze, near Venice, Leucos quickly established itself as a leader in the field of decorative lighting. The company's name, derived from the Greek word "leukos," meaning "light" or "brightness," reflects its commitment to creating luminous and innovative lighting solutions.

Throughout its history, Leucos has been renowned for its collaboration with prominent designers and architects, both Italian and international, to create iconic and avant-garde lighting designs. This collaborative approach has resulted in a diverse range of products that combine artistic flair with technical excellence. 

Over the decades, Leucos has continuously evolved, embracing new technologies and materials while staying true to its tradition of craftsmanship and quality. The company's dedication to innovation has led to the development of groundbreaking lighting concepts, including the use of Murano glass, LED technology, and customizable lighting solutions. 

Today, Leucos remains at the forefront of the lighting industry, offering a wide range of products that cater to various architectural and interior design needs. From elegant pendant lamps to sophisticated chandeliers, Leucos continues to illuminate spaces around the world with its timeless designs and unparalleled craftsmanship.

column2: Angelo Brotto Esperia Wall Lamp: Mid-Century Modern Italian Lighting
column3:
Introduce a statement piece to your interior with the imposing wall lamp designed by Angelo Brotto for Esperia in Italy during the 1970s.

This substantial fixture embodies the essence of mid-century modern design and represents a quintessential example of vintage Italian lighting. Angelo Brotto, a prominent figure in the world of Italian design, was known for his innovative approach to lighting and his ability to seamlessly blend form and function. His creations often feature bold shapes and luxurious materials, reflecting a timeless elegance that continues to captivate enthusiasts of design. Esperia, the esteemed Italian lighting company, was renowned for its collaboration with visionary designers like Brotto. Founded on principles of quality craftsmanship and artistic expression, Esperia produced a range of lighting fixtures that showcased the finest Italian design and manufacturing techniques. This wall lamp, with its impressive size and striking design, is a testament to the creative synergy between Brotto and Esperia. It exudes a sense of grandeur and sophistication, making it a captivating focal point in any space, while serving as a testament to the enduring legacy of mid-century Italian design.

Requires 4 x E27 bulbs and is suitable for both 220 and 110 voltages.

column2: Vintage ceramic bottle by Velimir Dordevic, Serbia, 1970s
column3:
Vintage ceramic bottle by Velimir Dordevic made in Serbia in the 1970s.

Velimir Dordevic - representative of the Serbia Pirot pottery school, whose activities span half a century of craft and art. This is a bottle for water or wine, in the Serbian tradition it is most often called “buklija” or “čutura” It was used for storing liquids, most often wine, raki or water. It was actively used at weddings, baptism and other ceremonies, by shepherds and peasants in the field, on holidays and during travels. It often had two necks: one for pouring, the second for drinking (or for regulating the air during pouring). The small legs allowed the vessel to be placed vertically. Green glaze with a characteristic spiral and leaf pattern is a traditional decorative motif from southern Serbia. The ornament can have a symbolic meaning - circles (spirals) often mean eternity, protection, the sun.

The vase is in very good vintage condition, with no damage.

The bottle has the original signature of the master.

Dimensions:

column2: Carlo Nason Murano Glass Pendant Lamp - Mazzega Italian Lighting 1960s
column3:
Very rare elegant Murano glass lamp by Carlo Nason for Mazzega. Recognized by connoisseurs as a true iconic design. Made in Italy in the 1960s.

Italian designer Carlo Nason was born into a family of expert glassmakers in 1935 in Murano, Venice. His father, Vincenzo Nason, directed the renowned glass business NasonMoretti and opened a second glass business under the name Vincenzo Nason & C in 1941. From an early age, the Carlo Nason received training in the art of glassblowing, learning about both the properties of the material and the artisanal techniques used in glass molding and decoration.

Two glasses are made in the "Pulegoso" technique and are assembled on a metal suspension. The "pulegoso" glass is made using a method that was often used in the 1920s, with the addition of gasoline to thereby cause the glass to boil in a melt. Countless tiny air bubbles of irregular shape inside the glass mass deliberately limit its transparency.

 Very good vintage condition. On the top glass there is a small chip that mostly isn't seen when the lamp is assembled (check the last photo).

The wiring is suitable for all countries in the world.

  • Width: 55 cm
  • Height: 42 cm

Maximum height with cable 120 cm.

The chandelier weighs 9 kg.

column2: Vintage table lamp Peacock by Riccardo Marzi RM Made in Italy - Firenze, 1980s
column3:
Introducing the rare and exquisite Peacock table lamp, crafted by Riccardo Marzi RM during the vibrant 1980s era. This lamp is a true marvel, boasting a lampshade made from clear resin infused with real dried leaves and herbs, creating a stunning representation of a peacock's majestic tail. The peacock itself is crafted from brass, adding to the lamp's allure and elegance. Marked with the signature "Riccardo Marzi RM Made in Italy - Firenze," this lamp bears the stamp of authenticity and craftsmanship. The company, renowned for its exceptional creations, describes its work as "works of high handmade craftsmanship," each containing the poetry and magic of nature's colors. Inspired by the breathtaking landscapes of Tuscany, each lamp in the collection encapsulates a small natural microcosm, reflecting the beauty of the region's hills, alleys, skies, and rivers. From ears of wheat to leaves, every material incorporated into these lamps undergoes a transformation, reborn into a vibrant and sophisticated object that pays homage to nature's splendor. Notably, the company's products have graced the prestigious halls of the MoMA in New York, a testament to their artistic merit and cultural significance. column2: Original Antique silver plated biscuit box by James Deakin & Sons, Sheffield, England, 1890s
column3:
Original Antique silver plated biscuit box by James Deakin & Sons made in Sheffield, England, the end of the 19th century. The company was founded in Sheffield in 1865 by James Deakin. column3:
Gorgeous Murano glass chandelier by Sylcom made in Italy in the 1990s. The manufacture Sylcom was founded in Venice in the 1950s and continues to work to this day, preserving the ideas and technologies of the Venetian glassblowers. Murrine is one of the most complicated and time-consuming murano techniques. First, the artist rolls a central rod in many differently colored glass liquids. When this rod is cut transversely into thin pieces, they display multicolored cross-sections. These cross-sections are arranged on a surface and the blown and shaped objects are rolled over these, while hot. The multicolored pieces stick to the surface of the object to give it an extremely picturesque look. The murrine glass is admired and valued greatly all over the world. It is also known as ‘millefiori’.

Excellent vintage condition!

column2: Vintage Elkington & Co Silver Plated Tankard: 1962 Trust Houses Mug
column3:
Vintage silver plated tankard 1 pint by Elkington & Co, England, 1962. Elkington & Co are one of the most important names in English Silver and Silver Plate. Elkington started in Birmingham in 1836, and experimented with innovative gilding techniques. By 1838 they had discovered and patented a new way to electroplate one metal on to the surface of another. By 1840 production was already underway with silver electroplated wares. The company received financial backing from Josiah Mason in 1842 (renaming the firm Elkington, Mason & Co between 1842 and 1861) and was extremely successful. It introduced electrotyping as a new method of production for silver plated items. Elkington & Co exhibited at the Great Exhibition of 1851 with enormous success. Elkington held Royal Warrants for Queen Victoria, King Edward VI, King George V, King Edward VIII and King George VI. The Elkington & Co. name is still in use today as manufacturers under the auspices of British Silverware Ltd. Trust Houses, the client for whom this mug was made, was part of a group that operated a chain of hotels and inns in the UK, with a focus on offering consistent quality and hospitality standards. This collaboration suggests that the mug was likely produced for use in one of these establishments, possibly as part of a larger set for dining or bar service. The year 1962 places it in a post-WWII period, during which Elkington & Co. was known to produce items both for commercial and private use. Silver-plated items from Elkington with clear markings, especially those produced for companies like Trust Houses, are quite collectible.

The antique beer mug has the original manufacturer's label.

Renato Toso (1940) and Noti Massari (1939), both Venetians by birth, culture, and choice, received their architecture degrees from Carlo Scarpa's (1906-1978) class in 1967 and got hitched the following year. Since then, they have built an architecture, engineering, and design studio inside the Venetian Lagoon with the help of their kids. At the Fratelli Toso 1854 glass works, which was a pioneer in embracing art glass after the fall of the Republic of Venice, they gained their first experience. They gradually started working with different designers, bringing the Murano island's skill to mass manufacturing. Their skills have been put to use on lighting projects for Vistosi, Artemide, Foscarini, Venini, and Leucos, among other Italian furniture and interior design firms. Many conferences, performances, and exhibitions, including those at the Palazzo Grassi and the Querini Stampalia Foundation in Venice, the Louvre in Paris, and the Museum of Modern Art, have featured them. The Palazzo Grassi and the Querini Stampalia Foundation in Venice, as well as group exhibitions at the Louvre in Paris, the Museum of Modern Art, and the Corning Glass Museums in New York, are just a few of the conferences, presentations, and exhibitions in which they have taken part. The first prize at the Venice Biennale, prizes at the Milan Triennale's 12th edition, and the national design competition in Trieste are among the honors they have received. They have been creating architectural plans and carrying out construction work for a Benedictine monastery in Tanzania since 2002.

This magnificent glass product was designed by Hana Machovska as part of the Romana line for the Mstislav glass factory, a renowned Czech glassblower with a history dating back to the 16th century. The Mstislav factory became part of the Sklo Union when it was founded in 1948.

The glass features a pink color on one side, a honey yellow in the middle, and a blue on the other side. It dates back to the 1950s and 1960s, and this series was considered to be the author's most significant design. It was described in Mark Hill's book, "Sklo: Czech Glass Design of the 1950s and 1970s."

Vintage glass ashtray is in very good vintage condition.

column2: Gino Sarfatti large globe chandelier Italy 1960s
column3:
Amazing chandelier by Gino Sarfatti made in Italy in the 1960s.

Made out of a metal structure holding 3 milk glass globes. Ball diameter 30 cm. Each ball consists of two parts - transparent and milky white. Gives a beautiful diffuse light emission.

Very good vintage condition. Requires three E27 bulbs. The wiring is suitable for all countries in the world. Wiring replaced by professional electrician.

Gino Sarfatti played a significant role in the history of industrial design in Italy. Italian engineer Gino Sarfatti started the Arteluce Company in 1939 and would go on to design over 700 lighting products—floor lamps, chandeliers, spotlights, and other light fittings—between the mid-1930s and early 1970s. His work at Arteluce effectively shaped the modern architectural movement in Italy and supported many of the mid-century designers including Franco Albini, Gianfranco Frattini, Sergio Asti, and Ico Parisi. Through his exploration and experimentation with new materials like Plexiglas, diverse production methodologies and techniques as well as light sources, such as halogen bulbs, Sarfatti was a true innovator of Italian lighting and design. The elegance chandelier by Gino Sarfatti lies in its ability to create a dazzling, starry sky-like effect in any room. Whether you're looking to add a touch of retro charm to your mid-century modern home or simply want to make a statement, this chandelier is sure to impress. The chandeliers by Gino Sarfatti is still highly sought-after by collectors, designers, and antique dealers alike. Its popularity is a testament to Sarfatti's ingenuity and design prowess, which allowed him to create a true piece of art that has stood the test of time.

  • Height: 100 cm

column2: Gino Sarfatti murano glass globe chandelier Italy 1960s
column3:
Amazing chandelier by Gino Sarfatti made in Italy in the 1960s. Made out of a metal structure holding 3 milk glass globes. Ball diameter 30 cm. Gives a beautiful diffuse light emission. The height of the chandelier can be adjusted from 77 cm to 110 cm with a special adjustment. Very good vintage condition. Chrome has damage. Requires three E27 bulbs. The wiring is suitable for all countries in the world. Gino Sarfatti played a significant role in the history of industrial design in Italy. Italian engineer Gino Sarfatti started the Arteluce Company in 1939 and would go on to design over 700 lighting products—floor lamps, chandeliers, spotlights, and other light fittings—between the mid-1930s and early 1970s. His work at Arteluce effectively shaped the modern architectural movement in Italy and supported many of the mid-century designers including Franco Albini, Gianfranco Frattini, Sergio Asti, and Ico Parisi. Through his exploration and experimentation with new materials like Plexiglas, diverse production methodologies and techniques as well as light sources, such as halogen bulbs, Sarfatti was a true innovator of Italian lighting and design. The elegance chandelier by Gino Sarfatti lies in its ability to create a dazzling, starry sky-like effect in any room. Whether you're looking to add a touch of retro charm to your mid-century modern home or simply want to make a statement, this chandelier is sure to impress. The chandeliers by Gino Sarfatti is still highly sought-after by collectors, designers, and antique dealers alike. Its popularity is a testament to Sarfatti's ingenuity and design prowess, which allowed him to create a true piece of art that has stood the test of time.

  • Height: 110 cm

This is a Venetian mirror, born in collaboration with Fabrica, a mirror that reflects images only if they are stationary, and movable ones are invisible. column2: Vintage Japanese Lithophane Geisha porcelain coffee set of 5, 1950s
column3:
Beautiful coffee set for 5 people. Marking: under glaze black stamp in the form of a hieroglyph. Dating: Japan, 1950s. At the bottom of the cups is a lithophane with a portrait of a geisha. These beautiful lithophane cups were first created in the late 1800s. The cups are thin, and the picture placed on the bottom of the cup is made using porcelain of various thicknesses. Most of these porcelain cups are made in the Kutani region of Japan. Be sure to check the bottom of the cup for light! If you hold it up to the light, you can see the lithophane of a geisha girl at the base of the cups. Lithophane (or lithopane or in French: lithophanie) is an etched or molded work of art on thin translucent porcelain that can only be clearly seen when illuminated from behind by a light source.

The security is good. No damage, no chips.

column2: Vintage pink vase by Archimede Seguso, Italy 1950s
column3:
Beautiful pink murano vase by Archimede Seguso made in Italy in the 1950s. Archimede Seguso was an Italian glass artist known for his exquisite glass vases, necklaces, and sculptures. His work spanned a wide range of shapes and colors, including animals and abstract forms. Born in Murano, Italy in 1909, he was a member of a long line of Italian glassmakers that had been creating exquisite glassware for over six centuries. Seguso led a revival of the ancient Venetian technique of filigrana, a lattice-like pattern, in his work. He ran the family business, Seguso Vetri d'Arte, from 1937 to 1942. The company continues to create beautiful glassworks that are now part of the collections of renowned museums such as The Museum of Modern Art in New York and the Victoria and Albert Museum in London. It is very well-preserved, It represents the patina expected from a glass object over 50 years old.

Since 1961, he worked alongside G. Gramigna at the Smc studio, focusing on furniture and industrial design. Among the many awards and honors he received, we can mention the Compasso d'Oro award in 1960 for the Delta lamp by Artemide, and the silver medal for Italian Apartment at the Triennale. From 1967, he also began designing for Olivari, creating the Macco handles for the Giuliana model with Gramigna. In addition, he directed the "Ottagono" magazine with Gramigna from its inception until 1988 in the publishing field.

After learning the woodworking and furniture manufacturing process at the Munich school, he moved to Milan in 1953 and worked in Gio Ponti's office. His clothes hook presented at Kartell in 1975 was chosen as the permanent collection of MoMa. Silver plated is used to denote - the method was invented in the city of Sheffield in about 1743 by goldsmith Thomas Boulsover. Objects made with the metal obtained by hot rolling unroll silver leaves on copper. This process fell into disuse after the adoption of the system of Silver Plating (silver plating of the base metal obtained with a galvanic bath), which began in 1850. column2: Antique Silver Plate Crumb Scoop - Atkin Brothers, Sheffield
column3:
Antique silver plate crumb scoop by Atkin Brothers made in Sheffield, England, the end of the 19th century. The Atkin Brothers can trace their roots back to Sheffield silversmith, Thomas Law, who started in 1750. His sons, John and William, continued the business in 1824. John Oxley and Henry Atkin then joined the company in 1829 and took over the original firm. By 1840, the partnership dissolved and Henry continued the trade alone. After his death in 1853, his three sons, Harry Wright Atkin, Edward Thomas Atkin, and Frank Shaw Atkin, took over the company under the name of Atkin Brothers. They manufactured a wide range of silverware, plate, and cutlery, supplying big names like Thornhill and Hall & Co.

The antique crumb scoop has the original manufacturer's label. This product marking refers to the years 1853-1936s.

column2: Arte Muranese Geometric Murano Glass Vases - Italian Home Decor - Set of 2
column3:
This exquisite set of two geometric vases showcases the timeless artistry of Arte Muranese, a venture that originated in Murano in 1983 under the guidance of master glassblower Luciano Nicetto. Luciano, having spent over three decades in various prestigious Murano furnaces like Seguso Vetri d'Arte and Vincenzo Nason, decided to venture into independent business. This marked the beginning of a new era, characterized by a harmonious blend of artistic expression, refinement, and innovation. In 1990, Luciano's son, Gianni, joined the same artistic journey, guided by the example and teachings of his father. This continuity preserves the family tradition of working with Murano glass. The combination of the father's experience and the son's creativity at Arte Muranese results in the creation of modern, classic, unique, and exceptional vases and lamps. These pieces represent a fusion of innovative materials and colors, reflecting a seamless integration of traditional Murano craftsmanship with contemporary art. This set of geometric vases is not just a testament to skilled craftsmanship but also a celebration of the enduring legacy of Murano glass artistry. A true masterpiece, these vases exemplify the delicate balance between tradition and innovation that defines Arte Muranese.

Size: round - width 31, height 30, depth 11; rectangular - width 23, height 37, depth 11.

Amazing original vintage Kalmar chandelier with thick textured glass pendants — each one resembles elongated icicles. Handmade glass, metal mount with natural patina. Austria, the 1970s. This model was often found in hotels, theaters, and interiors of that era. Its light is soft, slightly diffused, and plays beautifully on the glass. J. Kalmar was founded in Austria at the end of the 19th century (around 1881) by Julius August Kalmar. At first, the company was engaged in metal forging and decorative elements, but soon switched to the production of chandeliers and lamps, combining traditional craftsmanship and modern design. In the second half of the 20th century, especially from the 1950s to the 1970s, Kalmar became known for its collaboration with leading architects and designers. It was during this period that the company began to produce unique chandeliers and sconces, often made of murano glass, brass and other noble materials.

column2: Vintage Murano Ice glass chandelier Italy 1970s Mid-century modern italian lighting
column3:
The absolutely fantastic vintage Murano glass chandelier made in Italy in the 1970s. Consists of glass plates of Murano glass, made by Italian glassblowers. They follow the oldest Murano glassmaking technologies that were invented on the island of Murano. Each plate is unique because it is handcrafted. The Ice glass objects are distinguished by their cracked-look. The whole object will resemble cracked ice pieces. This effect is obtained by dipping the hot glass objects in cold water to crack their surfaces first, and later covering these cracks with another layer of molten glass. Then the cracks will appear through a layer of clear glass, making the object look as if it is made of ice. That is why it is also referred to as ice glass.

Chandelier in very good vintage condition.

column2: Murano pendant lamp by Carlo Nason for Mazzega Italy 1960s
column3:
Presenting an undeniably elegant and exceptional work of artistry, a Murano glass lamp by the renowned Italian designer Carlo Nason. This lamp stands as a truly rare find, acknowledged by connoisseurs as an iconic design that eloquently showcases the mastery and craftsmanship of Murano glassmakers. Born in 1935 in Murano, Venice, Carlo Nason comes from a lineage of expert glassmakers. His father, Vincenzo Nason, founded the esteemed glass business NasonMoretti and later established another glass enterprise named Vincenzo Nason & C in 1941. From a tender age, Carlo Nason underwent comprehensive training in the art of glassblowing, acquiring an intimate understanding of the distinct properties of glass and the intricate techniques involved in molding and embellishing it. Three of the glasses have been meticulously crafted using the "Pulegoso" technique, a method frequently employed in the 1920s. During the glassmaking process, gasoline is skillfully introduced into the molten glass, causing it to fervently boil and generate a multitude of minuscule air bubbles in irregular shapes. This intentional inclusion of unconventional bubbles artfully obscures the transparency of the glass, culminating in an enchanting visual effect. This vintage lamp is presented in excellent condition, allowing the impeccable quality of the glass to shine. The wiring has been expertly designed to accommodate usage across all countries worldwide. With a maximum height, cable included, of 150 cm, this lamp commands a truly impressive presence wherever it graces. Don't allow this extraordinary opportunity to slip through your grasp; seize the chance to own this exceptional piece of Murano glass art, a creation meticulously brought to life by none other than Carlo Nason himself. This lamp is destined to be a captivating addition to any collection, effortlessly asserting itself as a striking focal point within your home or office decor.

Silver plated is used to denote - the method was invented in the city of Sheffield in about 1743 by goldsmith Thomas Boulsover. Objects made with the metal obtained by hot rolling unroll silver leaves on copper. This process fell into disuse after the adoption of the system of Silver Plating (silver plating of the base metal obtained with a galvanic bath), which began in 1850. Such a processing system was commonly called Silver Plate.

column2: Antique Silver Plated Coffee and Tea Set by Elkington & Co, 1880s
column3:
Antique silver plated coffee and tea set by Elkington & Co made in the United Kingdom in the 1880s. Elkington & Co are one of the most important names in English Silver and Silver Plate. Elkington started in Birmingham in 1836, and experimented with innovative gilding techniques. By 1838 they had discovered and patented a new way to electroplate one metal on to the surface of another. By 1840 production was already underway with silver electroplated wares. The company received financial backing from Josiah Mason in 1842 (renaming the firm Elkington, Mason & Co between 1842 and 1861) and was extremely successful. It introduced electrotyping as a new method of production for silver plated items. Elkington & Co exhibited at the Great Exhibition of 1851 with enormous success. Elkington held Royal Warrants for Queen Victoria, King Edward VI, King George V, King Edward VIII and King George VI. The Elkington & Co. name is still in use today as manufacturers under the auspices of British Silverware Ltd.

The antique silver service has the original manufacturer's label.

Very good vintage condition.

The mid-century amber Murano chandelier by Toni Zuccheri for VeArt Scorze Venezia was made in Italy in the 1970s. Veart is founded in the 1965 by the collaboration of Sergio Biliotti and Ludovico Diaz de Santillana. A few years later, the master Diaz also becomes the artist's director of the famous Venini glass factory. He graduated in architecture in Venice, and he started teaching at the university. He married Anna Venini, the daughter of Paolo, the founder of the Venini glass company. The company worked in collaboration with international artists and designers such as Toni Zuccheri, Umberto Riva, Tobia Scarpa and many others. VeArt produces blown artistic glass until the 90s, when its brand was purchased and incorporated by Artemide. The glass parts are made of blown-thick Murano glass that has amazing colors.

column2: Nuvola Cocoon Chandelier by Tobia Scarpa for Flos, Italy 1962 Iconic Italian Mid-Century Modern
column3:
Step into the captivating world of Italian Mid-Century Modern design with the Nuvola cocoon chandelier by Tobia Scarpa for Flos in 1962. This light suspension model boasts an internal metal rod frame, gently embraced by a cocoon covering. Crafted by Tobia Scarpa for Flos in 1962, the chandelier takes its name, "Nuvola," inspired both by its cloud-like form and the delicate light that filters through the cocoon. Originating in mid-20th century Italy through Albert Eisenkeil's innovation, the cocoon technique adds an ethereal charm to national design history. Following this innovation, Achille, Pier Giacomo Castiglioni, and Tobia Scarpa joined the experimentation, leading to the birth of Flos in 1962. This pioneering company produced cocoon lamps that soon became refined icons of Italian design. Elevate your space with the iconic presence of Nuvola. Let this chandelier's unique blend of form, cocoon technique, and timeless design narrative cast an enchanting light that embraces the past while illuminating your present. Own a piece of Italian design history that beautifully marries artistry and innovation, defining an era of elegance.

column2: Murano table lamp Idra by Rosanna Toso for Leucos, Italy 1980s
column3:
Rosanna Toso, a distinguished member of the Toso family, played a pivotal role in the rich history of Murano Island, where the renowned Barovier Toso company was among the pioneers. Alongside her brothers, she dedicated her expertise to another esteemed family venture, "Fratelli Toso" (1854-1983), where she assumed the role of director from the mid-1970s until the company's dissolution in 1983. Following this chapter, Rosanna transitioned into the realm of light design, contributing her talents to the prestigious Leucos. One of her most acclaimed creations is the captivating "Idra" series from 1982, which manifested in various sizes and colors, gracing interiors as exquisite table lamps and sconces. The artistic technique employed in these masterpieces is known as Sommerso. This method intricately layers different glass sheets to achieve both thickness and a mesmerizing illusion of depth within the glass. Embedded within these layers are meticulously placed murrines, adding a touch of artistry that creates the visual illusion of a cascading waterfall, flowing seamlessly from the apex to the base of the globe.

  • Width: 40 cm
  • Height: 42 cm
  • Depth: 18 cm

column2: Murano glass pendant lamp  Italy 1960s Space age Mid-century modern italian lighting
column3:
The rare large hand-blown Murano glass pendant lampa made in Italy in the 1960s.

A transparent glass shade with a "Pulegoso" treatment that adds precious texture and a milky white "Lattimo" stripe around its entire circumference. The "pulegoso" glass is made using a method that was often used in the 1920s, with the addition of gasoline to thereby cause the glass to boil in a melt. Countless tiny air bubbles of irregular shape inside the glass mass deliberately limit its transparency. "Lattimo" is a special glass processing. This is a white milky-like glass in which the opacity is provided by the presence of microcrystals. The crystals change the index of refraction, thus giving the glass mass a milky appearance.

Excellent vintage condition. The metal has a natural patina. 


Dimensions:
Width: 40 cm / 15.7 inches
Height: 43 cm / 17 inches

Maximum height with cable 92 cm.

column2: Mid-century cocoon pendant lamp, Italy 1960s
column3:
Mid-century cocoon pendant lamp made in Italy in the 1960s.

The key feature of this iconic hanging lamp by architect Achille Castiglioni is the new material used to make it. The frame is metal covered in Cocoon spray-on plastic. In the United States, after World War II, a new type of fiberglass-reinforced spray-on plastic coating called Cocoon was invented to protect disarmed warships. This innovative material was imported to Italy by Arturo Eisenkeil, a small manufacturer from Merano, which used this resin to make lampshades and then put it on the market.

Vintage cocoon is very good vintage condition, no damage. Wiring replaced.

column2: Antique ceramic vase Horses by Egisto Fantechi Maioliche Ceramic, Sesto Fiorentino, Italy, 1940s
column3:
Rare vintage ceramic vase Horses by manufacture Egisto Fantechi Maioliche Ceramic made in Italy in the 1940s.

The company was founded in 1896 by a group of talented artists, including Egisto Fantechi. In 1905, after several years of collaboration, Egisto, who was the only true businessman in the group, took matters into his own hands and purchased all the other partners' shares. After gaining full control, he renamed the company Manufattura Egisto Fantechi. To maintain its recognizability, he kept the compass symbol within the company's shield.

The company continued until 1961, when it closed its doors for good.

The vase has the original manufacturer's label.

The vintage vase is in excellent vintage condition, with no damage.

column2: Table lamp Alfetta by Sergio Mazza for Artemide, Italy 1966, Set of 2
column3:
Pair of table lamp Alfetta by Sergio Mazza for Artemide in Italy in the 1966. The lamp uses Holophane glass.

The term "Holophane" is used in a general sense to describe transparent molded glass or crystal shades and globes with parallel or transverse prisms. Clear clean lines create a beautiful glow when illuminated. Holophane glass was invented in the 1890s and has been manufactured in many shapes, sizes and patterns to provide specific types of lighting. The term "Prismatic" is an alternative term for holophane glass. Sergio Mazza was born in Milan in 1931. He soon became a designer and in 1955 began his freelance career. Since 1961, he worked alongside G. Gramigna at the Smc studio, focusing on furniture and industrial design. Among the many awards and honors he received, we can mention the Compasso d'Oro award in 1960 for the Delta lamp by Artemide, and the silver medal for Italian Apartment at the Triennale. From 1967, he also began designing for Olivari, creating the Macco handles for the Giuliana model with Gramigna. In addition, he directed the "Ottagono" magazine with Gramigna from its inception until 1988 in the publishing field. 

The lamp is in very good vintage condition.

column2: Antique silver on copper teapot by Theodore B. Starr, USA, end of the 19th century
column3:
Antique silver on copper teapot by Theodore B. Starr made in the USA, end of the 19th century. Theodore B. Starr was a company of silversmiths founded in New York in 1862 by Theodore Starr. In 1864, he was joined by Herman Marcus, and the company became known as Starr and Marcus. Marcus left to join Tiffany's in 1877, and Starr bought back control of the company, with the name of the company becoming Theodore B. Starr. The company was incorporated in 1907 by Starr's son, before being bought in 1918 by Reed and Barton, a silver firm, and finally closing in 1923. The antique silver teapot has the original manufacturer's label. The lid doesn't fit snugly.

column2: Pair of Pink Murano Glass Table Lamps: Italian Mid-Century Lighting
column3:
Introducing a stunning pair of table lamps that exude a touch of 1980s Italian elegance – a fantastic duo crafted from pink Murano glass, expertly shaped in the form of a woman's hat. Created in Italy during the 1980s by Luci, these lamps stand as both functional illuminators and exquisite pieces of art. The lampshades, fashioned from pink Murano glass using the "Murrine" technique, showcase the mastery and tradition of glassmaking that dates back over 400 years. The captivating patterns and abstract images, revealed through thin cross-sections, add a unique and vibrant charm to these pieces. "Murrine" is a glassmaking process that originated in the Middle East centuries ago and was later revived by Venetian glassblowers on the renowned island of Murano. The result is a pair of table lamps that not only illuminate but also celebrate the rich history and craftsmanship of Murano glass. In excellent vintage condition, these lamps require an E14 bulb and are compatible with both 220 and 110 voltages. The maximum cable length of 145 cm provides flexibility in placement. As a delightful addition, there is also a large table lamp available, offering the opportunity to create a cohesive and harmonious lighting ensemble in your space. Illuminate your surroundings with the timeless beauty and artistic flair of these Murano glass table lamps by Luci.

Dimensions:

  • Width: 22 cm / 8.66 inches
  • Height: 28 cm / 11.02 inches

column3:
"The Venetian lagoon in the heavenly glare", Luigi Targa, Italy 1970s, Oil on Canvas, Framed Luigi Targa (1948-2014), an azure-colored artist, associated with Italy's "Nuova Scuola Romana" (New Roman School), created figurative works blending surreal and metaphysical elements. Potter and painter, educated at the Venice Academy of Fine Arts and a passionate teacher of art history and painting. Luigi Targa left hundreds of works, including one exhibited in the Vatican. His work has also been exhibited in Vienna, New York, Nice, Paris, Lisbon and Brussels.

The artwork is in very good vintage condition. There is a signature of the author.

column2: Pink Murano Glass Table Lamp: Italian Mid-Century Lighting
column3:
Fantastic large table lamp in pink Murano glass in the shape of a woman's hat. Made in Italy in the 1980s by Luci. Lampshade made of pink Murano glass, made in the "Murrine" technique. "Murrine" is a glassmaking technology that first appeared in the Middle East over 400 years ago. This process, referring to the colorful patterns and abstract images made on a glass cane, which only reveal themselves when the rod is cut into thin cross-sections, was later revived by Venetian glassblowers on Murano.

column2: Vintage fruit tray by Riccardo Marzi RM, Made in Italy - Firenze, 1990s
column3:
Riccardo Marzi from Florence, Italy, came from a family of artists and worked in several studios in Florence in the late fifties. His curiosity about new materials from the very beginning forced him to perform the difficult feat of “inventing” new art. Inspired by fossil amber, Riccardo was fascinated by the idea of using the transparency of resin to create everyday objects that could endlessly preserve the beauty bestowed by Nature. The Tuscan landscape proved to be an inexhaustible source of inspiration: starting with the use of wheat ears, oak and poplar leaves, broom flowers, oats, etc. Each work by Riccardo Marzi takes from one to two weeks to create. The complex process of manual production takes time and effort, but that's what makes Riccardo Marzi's work so exciting and unique. Riccardo Marzi's works have been on display at MoMA for over 20 years.

The tray is in very good vintage condition. The tray has the original manufacturer's label.

column2: Brutalist black armchair by Boris Krstulović, Yugoslavia, Croatia, 1970s
column3:
The unique armchair made of genuine leather in the brutalist style.

This chair was created by the iconic Croatian architect Boris Krstulović and has never been mass-produced. In 1967-75, the Croatian architect Boris Krstulović worked on the creation of a complex of buildings for the Control Center of the Elektroslavonija energy company in the city of Osijek (Yugoslavia, Croatia). In addition to the drawings of the building itself, he also designed the interior. He designed and created four unique chairs for the Director's office (Vladimir Žanić).

Currently, only one surviving chair is known! 

Good vintage condition. There are traces of time and usage. Chrome has a patina.

column2: Mid-century murano glass chandelier Tronchi by Toni Zuccheri for Venini Italy 1960s
column3:
Illuminate your space with the timeless elegance of this Murano glass chandelier, a masterpiece crafted in Italy during the 1960s by the renowned designer Toni Zuccheri for Venini & Co. Venini & Co. played a pivotal role in the revitalization of Italy's high-end glass industry, collaborating with innovative modernist designers and skilled artisans on the historic island of Murano. Founded by Paolo Venini, the company invited forward-thinking designers like Toni Zuccheri to Murano's workshops, creating pieces that became synonymous with artistic excellence. This particular chandelier, consisting of 42 parts, is a testament to the collaborative genius of Venini & Co., showcasing the seamless integration of design and craftsmanship. In very good vintage condition, this chandelier exudes sophistication, with the chrome showing some minor signs of wear that add character to its history. The chandelier requires one standard E27 bulb and nine E14 bulbs, providing a balanced and warm illumination. With a maximum height of 125 cm, including the cable, and a weight of 23 kg, it becomes a striking focal point in any space. Adorn your interior with the legacy of Italian design, where Murano glass meets modernist innovation, and let this chandelier by Toni Zuccheri cast a timeless glow on your surroundings.

column2: Chrome and Glass Lens Chandelier by Oscar Torlasco Italy 1960s, Vintage home decor
column3:
Beautiful Italian chandelier designed by Oscar Torlasco made in Italy in the 1960s. Known for his lamps that diffuse soft light through optical glass lenses, the Roman designer Oscar Torlasco (1934 - 2004) established himself in the '50s and '60s designing for several Italian companies specializing in lighting, such as Esperia, Stilux, Stilkronen. Oscar Torlasco won the Italian design award, the “Compasso d’Oro” (Golden Compass) for his Genova 4053 street lighting in 1959. Metal, the common denominator of his pieces, is also used by Torlasco for lamps and chandeliers with a much more eclectic taste, generally combined with marble, Murano glass and crystal. Metal chandelier has 6 crystal glass “optic lens”.

The lamp is in very good vintage condition.

The lamp boasts a unique and elegant design, featuring a spherical white Murano glass shade accented with a striking black stripe. The contrast between the white glass and the bold black stripe creates a visually captivating effect, making it a standout piece of Italian lighting design. The spherical shape of the lampshade adds a touch of modernity while still retaining a timeless appeal. This table lamp is not only a source of light, but also a work of art that can enhance the aesthetics of any room. Its sleek and chic design makes it a versatile piece that can fit into various interior styles, from Mid-century Modern to contemporary.

The lamp is suitable for use with a standard bulb and can accommodate both 110V and 220V electrical systems. It comes with an EU-spec plug for your convenience. The wiring has been updated by a professional electrician. If you're looking for an iconic piece of Italian lighting that combines form and function, this Murano glass table lamp from the 1970s is an excellent choice. It can add a touch of sophistication and style to your living space.

column2: Murano Glass Lamp: Cinea by Giusto Toso for Leucos, 1970s Italian Lighting
column3:
The magnificent Murano glass lamp Cinea by Giusto Toso for Leucos. It was made in the 1970s in Italy. Lampshade in the shape of a witch's hat in hand-blown crystal Murano glass. Smooth transition from transparent to white. Leucos was founded in 1962 in Scorze near Venice in Italy, and then moved to nearby Salzano. Giusto Toso has been collaborating with Leucos since 1969, developing a collection of glass objects and modular lighting elements. His work is exhibited at the Corning Museum in New York and the Victoria and Albert Museum in London. Some of his lamps have been selected and exhibited by the Center Pompidou in Paris.

In very good condition.

The maximum height of the lamp is 130 cm.

For safety, the holder and wiring have been replaced. Lamp weight 5 kg. The wiring is suitable for all countries around the world.

column2: Mid-Century Murano Glass Chandelier by AV Mazzega, Italy 1970s
column3:
Mid-century ice Murano glass chandelier by AV Mazzega was made in Italy in the 1970s. Although the lamp is not signed, we are sure of the heritage: the form and the used materials, from blown Murano glass and chrome, are typical for Mazzega.

AV Mazzega was founded in 1946 by Angelo Vittorio Mazzega. Mazzega and has worked with many internationally renowned designers such as De Lucchi, Piva, Zuccheri over the years. Best known is Carlo Nason who grew up in one of the oldest families of Murano glass manufacturers. The glass parts are made of blown-thick Murano glass that has amazing colors.

The lamp requires 3 x E14 light bulbs (not included). Suitable for all countries in the world.

Good vintage condition. The chandelier weighs 4 kg. The metal has a nature patina. The total height of the chandelier is 80 cm. One glass has a small inconspicuous crack (see last photo).

column2: Mid-century murano glass chandelier Tronchi by Toni Zuccheri for Venini Italy 1960s
column3:
Illuminate your space with the enduring elegance of this Murano glass chandelier, a creation from the 1960s designed by Toni Zuccheri for Venini & Co., a prominent player in the revival of Italy’s high-end glass industry. Venini & Co. brought together innovative modernist designers and skilled artisans on the renowned island of Murano. Founded by Paolo Venini, the company's genius lay in collaborating with forward-thinking designers like Toni Zuccheri. Notable designers like Giò Ponti, Massimo Vignelli, and Tapio Wirkkala also contributed to Venini's legacy. This chandelier, comprising 40 parts, is a testament to the fusion of artistic vision and masterful craftsmanship. In very good vintage condition, the chandelier exudes sophistication, with minor chrome damage that adds character. It requires 6 x E14 bulbs, and the maximum height with the cable is 56 cm, extendable to 160 cm. Weighing 16 kg, it becomes a captivating focal point in any space. The wiring has been professionally replaced, ensuring safety and functionality. Embrace the beauty of Italian design with this Murano glass chandelier, a piece that seamlessly marries artistry, innovation, and craftsmanship from the 1960s.

column2: Antique silver plated teapot by Sheffield, England, 1890-1910s, Vintage home decor
column3:
Antique silver plated teapot made in Sheffield, England in the 1890-1910s. Silver plated is used to denote - the method was invented in the city of Sheffield in about 1743 by goldsmith Thomas Boulsover. Objects made with the metal obtained by hot rolling unroll silver leaves on copper. This process fell into disuse after the adoption of the system of Silver Plating (silver plating of the base metal obtained with a galvanic bath), which began in 1850. Such a processing system was commonly called Silver Plate.

The antique teapot has the original manufacturer's label. Very good vintage condition.

column2: Vintage Murano Glass Table Lamps: Italian Mid-Century Modern Sphere, Set of 2
column3:
This stunning pair of vintage Murano glass table lamps Vetri Murano 004, is a testament to the exquisite craftsmanship that Italy is renowned for. Created in the 1970s, these lamps are a fusion of art and functionality.
Vetri Murano 004 is associated with the company LAG srl, which was located on the island of Murano. However, it no longer exists at present. Apparently, there are plans to construct a hotel on the site of its former factory.
The beauty of these lamps lies in their elegant design. The white glass, expertly molded into gracefully curved spheres, creates a captivating visual effect. One can't help but be enamored by the play of light and shadow that these lamps produce.
A notable feature is the original label, attesting to their Murano origin. Murano glass has a long history of excellence, and these lamps proudly carry on that tradition. They are not only aesthetically pleasing, but also a testament to the artisanal skills passed down through generations.
Despite their age, these lamps are in very good vintage condition, free from any chips or cracks that might detract from their beauty. They are not just sources of illumination; they are works of art that can adorn your living space.
For those with an appreciation for fine craftsmanship and a desire to bring a touch of Italian elegance into their home, these Murano glass table lamps are a perfect choice. They require a standard Edison E14 bulb with a screw fitting and come with a generous 120 cm of wire length, complete with an EU plug. Illuminate your space with these exceptional pieces of Italian artistry.

column2: Vintage Naive Art "Sad Clown" Oil Painting by Oto Dobovišek, 1980s Yugoslavia
column3:
This oil painting, titled "Sad Clown," is a remarkable piece of Naive art created by Oto Dobovišek in Yugoslavia during the 1980s.

This folk art portrait beautifully captures the poignant expression of a clown, evoking deep emotions and storytelling through the artist's unique style. Artist: Oto Dobovišek (born in Maribor in 1920) Medium: Oil on Hardboard Style: Naive Art Era: 1980s Origin: Yugoslavia This enchanting painting showcases the essence of Naive art, characterized by its simplicity, vivid colors, and a child-like perspective. The sad clown's expressive face and intricate details draw you into a world of emotions and curiosity. The artist, Oto Dobovišek, skillfully combines bold brushstrokes with rich colors, creating a distinctive piece of Yugoslavian folk art that tells a unique story. The inscription on the back of the painting is in Slovenian: “December 27, 1989. Luka is 8 months old.” We are probably talking about the artist’s little grandson.

This vintage painting is in excellent condition for its age, showcasing the enduring quality of both the artwork and the artist's craftsmanship.

"Sad Clown" would make a striking addition to any art collection or home decor. It's an ideal choice for those who appreciate the charm and storytelling of Naive art. Don't miss the opportunity to own this exquisite piece of Naive art that brings forth the emotions and mystique of a sad clown. Add it to your collection or display it as a captivating focal point in your home. Note: Please keep in mind that colors may vary slightly due to differences in monitor settings.

Mid-century green plastic pendant lamp by Stilux Milano made in Italy in the 1972.

Stilux Milano was an Italian lighting company that was founded in Milan in 1946. The company was known for its high-quality and innovative lighting designs, which were produced during the mid-20th century. Stilux Milano was an important company in the world of Italian lighting design during the mid-20th century, and its innovative and modernist designs continue to be admired and collected by enthusiasts and collectors today.
